title: A Reproducible Benchmark of Relational Database Access-Layer Performance in Express.js: A Configuration-Specific Comparison on PostgreSQL and MySQL
description: Version 1.5.1 refreshes the derived tables and prose after an adversarial re-review: the MySQL coefficient-of-variation table (S1) is regenerated to match the re-run data (the re-run made the MySQL insert cells noisier, CV up to 15.9%), and the order-invariance, durability, and dispersion summaries are corrected to their v1.5.0 measurements. No raw measurement, seed, or benchmark result changed from v1.5.0. Version 1.5.0 re-runs the entire benchmark on the latest stable library versions as of 2026-07-15 (Prisma 5.22->7.8 Rust-free via JS driver adapters, Drizzle 0.45, TypeORM 1.1, MikroORM 7, Express 5, autocannon 8). Headline change: the Prisma 5.22 in-process Rust query engine that drew ~5 application cores is gone; on Prisma 7 no layer exceeds ~110% of one core and Prisma falls to mid-to-low throughput, retiring the earlier CPU-parity finding and demonstrating the paper thesis that access-layer rankings are perishable. All 90 primary cells and every secondary experiment re-measured; byte-equivalence re-verified. Version 1.4.1 refreshes the archived paper sources to match the submitted manuscript after a length reduction to meet the IST 15,000-word limit (repeated conclusions single-sourced; secondary access patterns, secondary experiments, and the factor table moved to the numbered supplement; references trimmed 70 to 65). No benchmark data, results, or numbers changed from v1.4.0. Replication package (benchmark harness + paper sources + raw results) for a neutral comparison of relational database access layers in an Express.js service: native drivers (pg, mysql2) with tuned prepared-statement baselines (pg-tuned, mysql2-tuned), a query builder (Knex), and six ORMs (Drizzle, Prisma, Sequelize, TypeORM, Objection, MikroORM), across PostgreSQL 18.4 and MySQL 9.7.1, over five access patterns (point read, keyset range scan, deep/nested fetch, aggregation, insert), reporting throughput (req/s) and tail latency (p50/p90/p97.5/p99) jointly. Version 1.2 (revision round 2) protocol: 25 independent replicates per cell (fresh server process per replicate, randomized cell order verified by an order-invariance A/B check, paired PRNG-seeded request streams, 12 s runs after an evidence-based 15 s warm-up), connection pool fixed at 10; byte-identical response verification across all layers and engines before measurement (TZ=UTC, canonical constructors); writes measured under each engine's DEFAULT durability from a physically rebuilt database state, with a symmetric relaxed-durability secondary; process-tree CPU accounting with separate database and generator CPU; GC and connection-pool observability. Controls and evidence: same-SQL deep-fetch control with server-side statement capture and per-layer wire-protocol disclosure plus EXPLAIN plans, coordinated-omission-corrected open-loop (constant-arrival) tail validation, deep-fetch fan-out sweep (0-500 comments), equal-CPU-budget (taskset) control, ten-minute sustained-load drift check, connection-scaling sweep (1-256), and a no-database Express baseline. One-command re-run against containerized (digest-pinned) or local engines. Version 1.2 adds the paired/blocked inferential analysis matching the randomized-block design (paired sign-flip permutation and Wilcoxon signed-rank on per-replicate log-ratios, paired TOST, blocked layer-by-engine interaction), combined application-plus-database CPU accounting, and three further experiments: a connection-pool-size sensitivity sweep, a single-host resource-isolation check with the application, database, and load generator on disjoint core sets, and a transactional multi-statement write (post plus five comments in one transaction). Version 1.3 (revision round 3) reframes the claims to what the design supports (the same-SQL control is a bounding contrast, not a mechanism decomposition; the primary p99 is the saturated closed-loop tail at a stated operating point), extends paired p99 inference to all patterns and both engines, promotes a compact CPU/resource table and a primary/secondary outcomes table into the main text, and adds four targeted experiments: a both-engine sub-saturation open-loop tail, an alternative eager-loading (join vs select-in) sensitivity check, direct MySQL performance_schema commit-flush instrumentation of the insert bottleneck, and a post-restart environmental-robustness check. It also adds unit tests for every statistical estimator, per-adapter idiomatic-choice documentation, and effective-server-configuration and license files. Version 1.4 (revision round 4) repositions the manuscript as a reproducible benchmark instrument and a configuration-specific comparison rather than a generalized library ranking; renames the primary p99 a saturated client-observed response time and adds a utilization-controlled open-loop experiment showing the saturated tail gap dissolves at matched utilization; adds a multi-worker (node cluster) experiment showing the native driver overtakes the query-engine ORM once every layer uses the otherwise-idle cores; adds a per-layer pool-size frontier on both engines, a mixed read/write workload, and a longer-run sensitivity check; adds a 95% bootstrap confidence interval to every per-pattern throughput cell, a controlled/uncontrolled-factor table, a per-layer rank table, and a pre-specified adapter-selection protocol with documentation citations; and CORRECTS an earlier claim: a replicated (all-layer, eight-run) fan-out sweep shows the deep-fetch spread is a roughly fixed multiplier across graph sizes, not a growing one, so the growth claim is removed. A numbered online supplement (Supplement Tables S1-S26) accompanies the manuscript.

creators.name: Miotk, Mateusz
creators.affiliation: Faculty of Mathematics, Physics and Informatics, University of Gdańsk
